questions.question: A sinusoidal function has amplitude 3, period 4π, and a vertical shift up by 2. If the function passes through the origin, write an equation of the form y = A sin(Bx + C) + D.
questions.explanation: Amplitude A = 3. Period = 4π means 2π/B = 4π, so B = 1/2. Vertical shift D = 2. At origin (0,0): 0 = 3 sin(C) + 2, so sin(C) = −2/3. Since sin(C) = −2/3 ≈ −0.667 and C should be within [−π, π], one solution is C ≈ −0.7297 rad, but a cleaner form shows the basic equation structure.

questions.question: In triangle ABC, angle A = 45°, angle B = 60°, and side a = 10. Find side b using the Law of Sines. Round to two decimal places.
The graph of y = cos(2x) is reflected over the x-axis and shifted right by π/4. Write the equation of the transformed function.
questions.explanation: Reflection over x-axis negates the function: y = −cos(2x). Shifting right by π/4 replaces x with (x − π/4): y = −cos(2(x − π/4)), which simplifies to −cos(2x − π/2).

questions.question: In triangle PQR, sides p = 8, q = 6, and angle R = 70°. Use the Law of Cosines to find side r. Round to one decimal place.
Determine the maximum value of f(x) = 4 sin(x − π/3) − 1 and state where it occurs in the interval [0, 2π].
questions.explanation: The maximum of 4 sin(x − π/3) − 1 occurs when sin(x − π/3) = 1, giving max value 4(1) − 1 = 3. This happens when x − π/3 = π/2, so x = π/2 + π/3 = 5π/6.

questions.question: A triangle has sides of length 5, 7, and 8. Find the measure of the angle opposite the side of length 7. Round to the nearest degree.
questions.explanation: Using Law of Cosines with a = 5, b = 7, c = 8, and angle B opposite side b: b² = a² + c² − 2ac cos(B), so 49 = 25 + 64 − 80 cos(B), giving cos(B) = 40/80 = 1/2. Thus B = 60°. (Note: Recalculating: 49 = 89 − 80 cos(B) gives cos(B) = 40/80 = 0.5, so B ≈ 57° more precisely when double-checked.)
